title: The Market
subtitle: A small light is still a light.

dialogue.text: The weekend market smells like cinnamon and old wood. Stalls stretch down the street in crooked rows. Music from somewhere. A man selling apples calls out prices nobody listens to.

dialogue.speaker: yen
dialogue.text: This place is HUGE.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Hold on — there he is.
Drew waves. Across the crowd, a man waves back. Tall. Steady. The kind of person whose presence makes a room feel organized. He moves through the market like he knows every stall by name.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Shepherd! Over here!

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: There they are. The whole crew.

He ruffles Yen's hair. She ducks and giggles. He nods at Lu — respectful, not condescending. He shakes Rush's hand like Rush is an adult. Then he clasps Drew's shoulder, and Drew clasps his. Brothers in everything but blood.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Shepherd's the one who told me about this market. He knows everyone here.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: Not everyone. But enough to find the good stuff. Who wants cotton candy?

dialogue.speaker: yen
dialogue.text: ME ME ME ME ME—
Shepherd buys Yen cotton candy without being asked. Carries Lu's bag when it gets heavy. Walks beside Drew, and they fall into conversation like they've been mid-sentence for years.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: You've got good kids, Drew. Hold onto that.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: They're not mine. I'm just the oldest.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: Same thing.
Drew smiles at that. The kind of smile that comes from being seen by someone you respect.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Okay. Rules. Nobody wanders off alone. We stick together, we meet back at the fountain in one hour. Got it?

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: Yes, dad.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: Listen to your brother. He's smarter than he looks.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Thanks for that.
Drew pulls out some crumpled bills. Counts them carefully. Splits them.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Rush — here's enough for one thing. Choose wisely.

dialogue.speaker: rush
dialogue.text: (Drew gave me his own money. He does that.)
Rush finds a woven bracelet at one of the stalls. Rose-colored thread. Lu's color. He buys it without thinking too hard.

dialogue.speaker: rush
dialogue.text: Here. I saw this and thought of you.

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: ...You spent your money on me?

dialogue.speaker: rush
dialogue.text: It's not a big deal.
It is, though. She puts it on immediately. Drew watches from across the stall. Shepherd leans over to Drew and says something Rush can't hear. Drew nods. Smiles.
Rush finds a pocket knife with a wooden handle. Small. Practical. He likes the weight of it.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: Good choice. A tool, not a toy. I had one just like that at your age.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Take care of it.
Rush puts the money in his pocket. Saves it. Doesn't know what for yet.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Smart. Patience is its own reward, Rush.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: Discipline. I like that.
Drew and Shepherd walk ahead. The kids trail behind. From here, Rush can hear fragments of their conversation. Easy. Familiar. The way people talk when they've known each other a long time.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: ...and you're handling it? All of it?

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: I'm managing.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: You don't have to manage alone. That's why I'm here.
Drew puts his hand on Shepherd's arm. Brief. Grateful. The kind of contact that says 'I know' without needing to.

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: Are they always like this?

dialogue.speaker: rush
dialogue.text: Like what?

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: Like... brothers.
She's right. They are. Drew has a brother that isn't Rush, and Rush never thought about what that means until this moment.
The stall is at the end of the row. Easy to miss. An old woman sits behind a table covered in lanterns — brass ones, glass ones, paper ones. All shapes. All sizes.

dialogue.speaker: yen
dialogue.text: Look at all the lights!
The old woman looks up. Her eyes find Drew immediately. She looks at him the way you look at something you recognize but haven't seen in a long time.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: I know this stall. She's been here for years. Good stuff.
The old woman's eyes don't move from Drew. Shepherd doesn't notice. Or doesn't show it.
Drew approaches the table. He picks up a small, plain thing. Not a lantern exactly. A holder — iron, simple, with a groove for a wick.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: What's this one?
The old woman smiles. Not warm. Knowing.
"A rushlight. People used them when they couldn't afford real candles. Animal fat and a reed wick. The poorest light there is."

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: How much?
She names a price. Drew pays without haggling.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: What do you want that old thing for?

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Think about it. Somebody made this because they needed light and couldn't afford it. They used what they had. Animal fat. A reed. And they made it work.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: A small light is still a light.
Shepherd looks at Drew. Something passes across his face — not mockery. Not agreement. Recognition. Like he's seeing something in Drew he already knew was there.
A black shape leaps onto the table. Kimiru. The old woman doesn't flinch.
She leans down to the cat. Whispers something. Her lips move but the words don't carry.
Kimiru listens. His ears flatten. Then he jumps down and walks away without looking back.
The old woman watches Drew walk away with the rushlight. She mouths two words. You can't read them. You want to.
Walking home. The sun is low. Drew carries the rushlight in his jacket pocket. Shepherd walks beside him. The kids trail behind, tired and full of market food.

dialogue.speaker: yen
dialogue.text: My feet hurt.

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: Hop on.
He crouches. Yen climbs onto his back. Shepherd takes Drew's bags without being asked. This is a thing they do. A system built from years.

dialogue.speaker: lu
dialogue.text: Drew, why did that woman look at you like that?

dialogue.speaker: drew
dialogue.text: I don't know. Maybe I remind her of someone.

dialogue.speaker: shepherd
dialogue.text: She's been there a long time. She looks at everyone like that.
Drew's hand goes to the pocket with the rushlight. And stays there. Shepherd notices. Says nothing.
Five people walking home in the last light. Four kids and a man who carries their bags and knows their names and calls Drew 'brother' without it being strange.
The kind of evening that feels like it could last forever. It can't. Nothing can. But they don't know that yet.
